content:
5 min read 14 hours ago -- Angular templates look like HTML. But the browser never executes expressions such as: {{ product.name }} @if (product.inStock) { ... } @for (item of items; track item.id) { ... }Those expressions only have meaning to Angular. An Angular template is based on HTML syntax, but it adds its own features for data binding, events, components, variables, and control flow. Angular processes that template and turns it into JavaScript instructions that create and update the DOM. So the more accurate mental model is: Angular template ↓ Angular compiler ↓ JavaScript rendering instructions ↓ Browser DOMIn a normal AOT-compiled Angular application, the browser receives the compiled application. It does not download your component template and interpret @if, *ngIf, or interpolation by itself. What happens to *ngIf? Consider the older structural-directive syntax:
Welcome
The * is shorthand. Angular expands it conceptually into:
Welcome
The
becomes the content of a template that Angular can insert or remove depending on the condition. This also explains a well-known Angular rule:
{{ item.name }}
Only one structural directive can use the shorthand syntax on an element because Angular would not know which directive should own the generated wrapper. The usual workaround is to introduce another template layer:
{{ item.name }}
groups template content without adding another normal HTML element to the rendered page. How modern control flow is different Angular introduced built-in control flow in Angular 17: @if (isVisible) { @for (item of items; track item.id) {
{{ item.name }}
} }@if, @for, and @switch are not directives imported from CommonModule. They are part of Angular’s template language itself. That is why they do not have the same “one structural directive per element” problem. They are blocks containing template nodes rather than directives competing for the same host element. However, it would be misleading to say that modern control flow has completely removed templates or embedded views internally. Angular still uses concepts such as embedded views and view containers to render conditional and repeated content. The difference is mainly that control flow is now understood directly by the compiler instead of being implemented through user-facing directives such as NgIf and NgFor. In current Angular versions, NgIf and NgFor are deprecated in favour of @if and @for. Why track matters in @for A loop is not only responsible for displaying items. Angular must also determine which rendered DOM view belongs to which data item. @for (order of orders; track order.id) { }Here, order.id acts as the identity of each row. When an order is added, removed, or reordered, Angular can associate the updated data with the correct existing DOM view and perform only the required DOM operations. For dynamic collections, prefer a stable unique property: @for (user of users; track user.id) { }For a collection that never changes order or content, Angular also allows: @for (day of weekdays; track $index) { {{ day }} }Tracking by $index is therefore not always wrong. It is suitable for static collections, but a stable identifier is safer when items can be inserted, removed, or reordered. What happened with *ngFor? With the older syntax, trackBy was optional:
{{ order.id }}
Without a custom trackBy, Angular used the object’s identity. That does not mean Angular recreated every DOM node whenever a new array object was assigned. For example, creating a new array that still contains the same object references may allow Angular to retain the existing views. The expensive case commonly appears after data is fetched again from an API: this.orders = response.orders;The new response may contain completely new object instances, even when the underlying orders have the same database IDs. Without trackBy, Angular can treat those objects as entirely new items. A stable tracking function solves that: trackOrder(_: number, order: Order): number { return order.id; }
{{ order.id }}
Modern @for makes the tracking decision explicit by requiring a track expression. Why this mental model matters Understanding template compilation helps explain several Angular behaviours: • Why Angular-specific syntax does not appear literally in the final DOM. • Why two * structural directives cannot be placed on one element. • Why is useful. • Why modern control-flow blocks do not need CommonModule. • Why stable tracking keys matter for dynamic lists. • Why template errors can be detected during the build. You do not need to think about compiler internals while building every component. But when a template behaves unexpectedly, knowing that Angular is compiling a template language — not handing Angular syntax directly to the browser — makes the framework much easier to reason about. Interview question Why can’t *ngIf and *ngFor be used on the same element? Because the * shorthand transforms the host element into content inside an . Only one structural directive can control that generated template. You can solve it by nesting elements, using , or replacing the legacy directives with modern @if and @for blocks. Key takeaway An Angular template is HTML-based, but it is more than HTML. The browser does not understand Angular’s interpolation, bindings, structural directives, or control-flow blocks. Angular compiles those features into the JavaScript responsible for creating and updating the DOM. That distinction explains much of Angular’s template behaviour. Have you already migrated your applications from *ngIf and *ngFor to @if and @for? Press enter or click to view image in full size
